text: Master endurance athletes (mean age >59, training duration ≥20 years) maintain VO2max values comparable to young healthy controls (42.0 vs 43.1 ml/kg/min, P=.84).
population: master endurance athletes (>59 years, ≥20 years training)

text: Master endurance athletes exhibit significantly higher VO2max than sedentary older controls (42.0 vs 27.1 ml/kg/min, P<0.01).
population: master endurance athletes vs older controls

text: Master endurance athletes have lower VO2max than young endurance-trained individuals (42.0 vs 60.0 ml/kg/min, P<0.01), indicating some age-related aerobic decline persists despite chronic training.
population: master endurance athletes vs young trained athletes

text: Chronic exercise training preserves muscular strength and physical function to levels similar to young healthy individuals, but the preservation is exercise mode-specific (strength/power athletes retain strength advantage, endurance athletes retain aerobic capacity).

Master endurance athletes maintain lower body fat percentage than sedentary older controls (7.11% lower, P<0.01) but have higher body fat than young endurance-trained individuals (4.44% higher, P<0.05).
specific_value: 7.11% lower than older controls, 4.44% higher than young trained

text: Master strength/power athletes retain significantly greater muscular strength compared to master endurance athletes and older controls, suggesting masters runners should incorporate strength training to preserve muscle function.
population: master strength/power athletes vs other master athlete groups

text: At least 20 years of consistent endurance training is associated with preservation of aerobic capacity, body composition, and physical function into the 7th decade and beyond.
